paper_title: ClawNet: Human-Symbiotic Agent Network for Cross-User Autonomous Cooperation

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Current AI agent frameworks have made remarkable progress in automating individual tasks, yet all existing systems serve a single user. Human productivity rests on the social and organizational relationships through which people coordinate, negotiate, and delegate. When agents move beyond performing tasks for one person to representing that person in collaboration with others, the infrastructure for cross-user agent collaboration is entirely absent, let alone the governance mechanisms needed to secure it. We argue that the next frontier for AI agents lies not in stronger individual capability, but in the digitization of human collaborative relationships. To this end, we propose a human-symbiotic agent paradigm. Each user owns a permanently bound agent system that collaborates on the owner's behalf, forming a network whose nodes are humans rather than agents. This paradigm rests on three governance primitives. A layered identity architecture separates a Manager Agent from multiple context-specific Identity Agents; the Manager Agent holds global knowledge but is architecturally isolated from external communication. Scoped authorization enforces per-identity access control and escalates boundary violations to the owner. Action-level accountability logs every operation against its owner's identity and authorization, ensuring full auditability. We instantiate this paradigm in ClawNet, an identity-governed agent collaboration framework that enforces identity binding and authorization verification through a central orchestrator, enabling multiple users to collaborate securely through their respective agents.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: 6 2 0 2 1 2 ] . [ 1 1 1 2 9 1 . 4 0 6 2 : r ClawNet: Human-Symbiotic Agent Network for Cross-User Autonomous Cooperation Zhiqin Yang*, Zhenyuan Zhang*, Xianzhang Jia*, Jun Song*, Wei Xue*, Yonggang Zhang , Yike Guo Hong Kong Generative AI Research & Development Center Hong Kong University of Science and Technology Hong Kong Baptist University GitHub: https://github.com/hkgai-official/ClawNet Project Page: http://www.clawnet.hk/ Current AI agent frameworks have made remarkable progress in automating individual tasks, and multi-agent systems have further demonstrated the potential of multiple agents collaborating toward shared objectives. Yet these systems invariably serve single user. Human productivity rests not on individual skills or experience alone, but on the social and organizational relationships through which people coordinate, negotiate, and delegate. When agents move beyond performing tasks for one person to representing that person in collaboration with others, the infrastructure for cross-user agent collaboration is entirely absent, let alone the governance mechanisms needed to secure it, including stable owner binding, scoped authorization, and auditable accountability. We argue that the next frontier for AI agents lies not in stronger individual capability, but in the digitization of human collaborative relationships themselves. To this end, we propose human-symbiotic agent paradigm. In this paradigm, each user owns permanently bound agent system that participates in collaboration on the owners behalf, collectively forming collaboration network whose nodes are humans rather than agents. This paradigm rests on three governance primitives. layered identity architecture separates Manager Agent from multiple context-specific Identity Agents. The Manager Agent holds global knowledge but is architecturally isolated from external communication, serving both as privacy safeguard and as an internal advisor to Identity Agents. Scoped authorization enforces per-identity access control and automatically escalates boundary violations to the owner. Action-level accountability logs every operation against its owners identity and the corresponding authorization, ensuring full auditability. We instantiate this paradigm in ClawNet, an identity-governed agent collaboration framework built on the OpenClaw framework. ClawNet enforces identity binding and authorization verification through central orchestrator during cross-user collaboration, preventing unauthorized access and information leakage, enabling multiple users to collaborate securely and efficiently through their respective agents. * Equal contribution Contact information: zhangyg@ust.hk, yikeguo@ust.hk ClawNet: Human-Symbiotic Agent Network for Cross-User Autonomous Cooperation

sections.title: Contents
sections.content: 1 Introduction 2 System Overview 2.1 Human-Symbiotic Collaboration Network . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 2.2 Design Rationale . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 2.3 Cloud-Side Overview . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 2.4 Node Endpoint Overview . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 1 3 3 5 5 6 3 7 Identity Governance and Agent Collaboration 7 Identity Binding: From Task Memory to Cognitive Coupling . . . . . . . . . . . 3.1 3.2 Layered Identity Architecture . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 8 3.3 Cross-User Collaboration . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 10 4 Related Work 5 Conclusion 11 11 ClawNet: Human-Symbiotic Agent Network for Cross-User Autonomous Cooperation 1. Introduction AI agent technology is advancing rapidly from conversational generation to autonomous execution (Wang et al., 2024; Guo et al., 2024; Qin et al., 2024; Yao et al., 2022). Early conversational systems were limited to text generation and could not interact with external environments (Achiam et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2024). Tool-augmented agents then introduced API-level interaction through function calling and tool-use mechanisms (Schick et al., 2023; Qin et al., 2024). More recently, OS-level agents such as OpenClaw (OpenClaw Community, 2026) and Anthropics Computer Use (Anthropic, 2024) have achieved direct control over files, terminals, and desktop applications, advancing single-agent capability from advice to action (Wang et al., 2024). In parallel, multi-agent systems such as MetaGPT (Hong et al., 2023), AutoGen (Wu et al., 2024), CrewAI (cre, 2024), LangGraph (lan, 2024), and ChatDev (Qian et al., 2024) have demonstrated that multiple agents can collaborate effectively toward shared objectives (Guo et al., 2024). Yet whether single-agent or multi-agent, these systems invariably serve single user (Figure 1, Left). Agents within existing multi-agent frameworks share the same principal, operate under unified goal, and do not need to represent distinct human interests (Guo et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024). As summarized in Table 1, none of the existing frameworks provide mechanisms for agents of different users to interact on behalf of their respective owners. This stands in sharp contrast to how humans actually work, where productivity rests not on individual skills alone but on the social and organizational relationships through which people coordinate, negotiate, and delegate (Malone et al., 2004; Coase, 1993). In any real-world collaboration, three structural elements are indispensable (Shavit et al., 2023). Identity establishes who participant is and whom they represent. Authorization defines what actions are permitted within given context and what must be escalated. Accountability ensures that every action can be traced back to the individual who acted and the mandate under which they acted. These elements are so deeply embedded in human collaboration that they are often taken for granted, yet none of them exists in current agent systems. Existing agent systems have successfully digitized individual skills (Wang et al., 2024; Qin et al., 2024), but none of these three elements has been addressed. Emerging interoperability protocols such as Googles Agent2Agent (Surapaneni et al., 2025) provide communication layer through which agents can discover and exchange messages across framework boundaries, but they do not bind agents to specific owners, enforce authorization scopes, or produce auditable responsibility chains. There is neither means through which different users agents can identify and interact with one another (Chan et al., 2025), nor governance mechanism to safeguard each partys interests during collaboration (de Witt, 2025). Agents lack stable owner binding and therefore cannot verifiably represent specific individual when initiating or participating in cross-user workflows (South et al., 2025; Shavit et al., 2023). Even if collaboration were to occur, no scoped authorization or auditable accountability exists to prevent unauthorized access, information leakage, or decisions made without mandate (Chan et al., 2025; Casper et al., 2025). Existing safeguards such as sandboxing, per-call permission prompts, and capability restrictions address single-user misuse but leave the structural risks of cross-user collaboration entirely unmitigated (Shavit et al., 2023; de Witt, 2025). To address this gap, we move beyond strengthening individual agent capability and focus instead on digitizing human collaborative relationships, equipping agents with the identity, authorization, and accountability infrastructure that makes cross-user collaboration via autonomous agents possible. To this end, we propose human-symbiotic agent paradigm. In this paradigm, each user owns an agent system that is permanently bound to its owner (Chan et al., 2025; South et al., 2025), much as social-media account is inseparably tied to the person who created it. The system comprises multiple identity agents, each corresponding to specific collaborative context, collectively covering the users collaboration needs across different social 1 ClawNet: Human-Symbiotic Agent Network for Cross-User Autonomous Cooperation Figure 1: Paradigm comparison between OpenClaw and ClawNet. (Left) In current frameworks, agents reside beneath their users as isolated executors with broad but undifferentiated resource access, no persistent identity, and no cross-user communication protocol. All interuser coordination falls to the humans themselves. (Right) In ClawNet, agents form governed collaboration layer above their owners. Each agent is permanently bound to one owner (identity binding), operates under explicit, revocable permissions (scoped authorization), and logs every action against its owners identity and mandate (action-level accountability). Humans retain intent formulation and critical decisions; all cross-user coordination is realized through structured inter-agent negotiation within this accountable layer. Table 1: Paradigm comparison across representative agent frameworks. Existing multi-agent systems coordinate agents under single principal; ClawNet binds each agent to its own owner and governs cross-user collaboration through identity binding, scoped authorization, and action-level accountability. = fully supported; = partially supported; = not supported. Paradigm Representative Exec. Level Identity Binding Scoped Auth. Action Account. Cross-Owner Collab. Conversational AI ChatGPT, Claude Chat Text gen. Tool-Augmented AI ChatGPT Plugins, MCP API inter. OS-Level Single Agent OpenClaw, Computer Use OS-level Single-Principal Multi-Agent MetaGPT, AutoGen, CrewAI, LangGraph, ChatDev Cross-Agent Protocol Google A2A HumanSymbiotic (Ours) ClawNet API / OS API-level OS-level and professional roles. The manager agent serves as the authoritative anchor of the users digital identity. Because its breadth of knowledge makes direct external exposure privacy risk, the manager agent is architecturally isolated from all cross-user communication, serving exclusively as an internal governor and advisor to identity agents. Each identity agent is context-specific projection of the owner, created and retired as the owners social and professional roles evolve. For example, user who takes on new managerial position may instantiate corresponding identity agent, designate which collaborators are authorized to interact with that identity, and dissolve it when the role ends. No single identity agent possesses the full extent of its owners knowledge; rather, it operates strictly under its designated identity, inheriting only the information relevant to that identity. It persists across sessions, progressively accumulates an understanding of its owners preferences and the collaborative dynamics of its context, and acts autonomously within its authorization boundary. Through this ensemble, users participate in cross-user collaboration via their identity agents. As shown in Figure 1, the resulting network is fundamentally different from existing multi-agent topologies: its nodes are humans, not agents, and each edge represents governed collaborative relationship rather ClawNet: Human-Symbiotic Agent Network for Cross-User Autonomous Cooperation than an inter-process message channel. We instantiate this paradigm in ClawNet, an open-source identity-governed agent collaboration framework. ClawNet adopts cloud-edge architecture: the manager agent and all identity agents run as persistent cloud services for always-on availability and cross-user orchestration, while lightweight edge client installed on the owners device serves as both the users interaction interface and local execution engine with OS-level control capabilities. The edge client further enforces file-level access control, granting different identity agents access to different subsets of local files according to their respective roles. Notably, the cloud and edge components can be co-located on single physical machine when fully local deployment is preferred. ClawNet has been deployed and tested across cross-organizational collaboration scenarios, demonstrating that the governance primitives operate effectively in practice. In summary, this paper makes the following contributions: We propose human-symbiotic agent paradigm that introduces three governance primitives (identity binding, scoped authorization, and action-level accountability) for cross-user agent collaboration, along with layered identity architecture separating privacy-preserving manager agent from multiple context-specific identity agents. We design and implement ClawNet, an open-source identity-governed agent collaboration framework featuring cloud-edge architecture, dual-layer independent file authorization, and comprehensive operational audit trail that ensures every agent action remains traceable and correctable by the owner. We validate ClawNet through representative cross-organizational collaboration scenarios, demonstrating that governance-aware agents can mediate multi-party negotiations while maintaining identity isolation, authorization enforcement, and full auditability. Through this instantiation, we advocate for broader shift toward human-symbiotic governance in the design of collaborative agent systems. 2. System Overview This section first formalizes the human-symbiotic paradigm as collaboration network with humans as nodes and governed agent interactions as edges (2.1), then discusses the rationale behind the cloud-edge architectural separation (2.2), and finally presents the cloud-side components (2.3) and the edge-side node endpoint (2.4) that jointly support this network. 2.1. Human-Symbiotic Collaboration Network ClawNet can be understood to social network with AI assistant. In conventional social network such as WeChat, people establish connections and exchange messages directly. In ClawNet, each user additionally owns permanently bound agent system, and collaboration between users is mediated and executed through their respective agents. This gives rise to collaboration network whose nodes are humans and whose edges are governed agent interactions. Collaboration network. Let 𝒰 be the set of users. ClawNet constructs collaboration network 𝒢 = (𝒰 , ℰ), where each node is human user 𝑢 𝒰 and each edge 𝑒(𝑢, 𝑣) ℰ represents governed collaborative relationship established through the agents of 𝑢 and 𝑣. Unlike existing multi-agent topologies, the nodes of this network are always humans, not agents. Agent system. Each user 𝑢 owns permanently bound agent system 𝒜𝑢 comprising 𝑢}. 𝑀𝑢 governs the creation, manager agent 𝑀𝑢 and set of identity agents {𝐼1 configuration, and retirement of all identity agents, holds complete knowledge 𝒦𝑢 of its owner, but is architecturally isolated from all external communication, accessible only by agents 𝑢 , . . . , 𝐼 𝑘 𝑢 , 𝐼2 3 ClawNet: Human-Symbiotic Agent Network for Cross-User Autonomous Cooperation Figure 2: Governance-aware cross-border collaboration scenario. procurement workflow between CN Tech Co. (buyer) and US Nova-Semi (supplier) is executed entirely through interagent negotiation. Steps 13: Mr. Li issues high-level procurement intent; his agent, operating under its bound identity and scoped authorization, reads local requirement documents and forwards structured request to the supplier CEOs agent. Steps 46: The CEOs agent decomposes the task within its own authorization scope, delegating technical evaluation and commercial analysis to two subordinate agents (Tech. Claw, Business Claw), each accessing only the local resources permitted by its respective owner. Private data such as costs and floor prices remain strictly within the suppliers boundary. Steps 78: Results are aggregated into proposal and returned to Mr. Lis agent, which escalates the decision to its owner for final approval. Step 9: Upon human authorization, the agent executes the remaining local operations. Throughout the workflow, every agent action is traceable to specific owner and authorization scope, unauthorized information requests are rejected by governance enforcement rather than agent discretion, and all decisions requiring human judgment are escalated rather than autonomously resolved. within 𝒜𝑢. By analogy, 𝑀𝑢 is the users primary account that is never exposed to the outside world. The design rationale and detailed mechanisms of this layered architecture are presented in Section 3. Identity agent. Each identity agent 𝐼 𝑖 𝑢 corresponds to specific collaborative context, analogous to an identity tag in social network. Formally, 𝐼 𝑖 𝑢 = (𝑐𝑖 , 𝜎𝑖 , 𝒦𝑖 , 𝒫𝑖) where 𝑐𝑖 is the collaborative context (e.g., procurement manager), 𝜎𝑖 ℛ𝑢 is the scoped authorization boundary defining the accessible resource subset, 𝒦𝑖 𝒦𝑢 is the contextrelevant knowledge inherited through the memory system and progressively accumulated across sessions, and 𝒫𝑖 𝒰 {𝑢} is the set of users authorized to interact with this identity. Only collaborators in 𝒫𝑖 can discover and interact with 𝐼 𝑖 𝑢, much as only designated contacts can see specific tag in social profile. 𝑢 locates the corresponding identity agent 𝐼 𝑗 Discovery and connection. When user 𝑢 wishes to collaborate with user 𝑣, the identity agent 𝐼 𝑖 𝑣 through the identity tags published by 𝑣 in the collaboration network. connection requires explicit authorization from both human owners: 𝑆(𝐼 𝑖 𝑢 , 𝐼 𝑗 𝑣) holds iff approve(𝑢, 𝐼 𝑖 𝑢) approve(𝑣, 𝐼 𝑗 𝑣) 𝑢 𝒫𝑗 𝑣 𝒫𝑖 The detailed collaboration lifecycle, including contact establishment, bilateral approval, multiturn dialogue, and manager consultation, is described in Section 3.3. Recursive collaboration. When 𝐼 𝑗 𝑣 discovers during collaboration that third party 𝑤 is 4 ClawNet: Human-Symbiotic Agent Network for Cross-User Autonomous Cooperation needed, 𝑣 may authorize 𝐼 𝑗 𝑣 to initiate new collaboration request to 𝑤, forming chain: 𝑆(𝐼 𝑖 𝑢 , 𝐼 𝑗 𝑣) 𝑆(𝐼 𝑗 𝑣 , 𝐼 𝑚 𝑤 ) Each level of recursion requires explicit authorization from the user at that level, and the recursion depth is bounded by system parameter 𝑑max. Crucially, upper-level collaborators cannot penetrate lower-level authorization boundaries: 𝐼 𝑖 𝑢 cannot directly access the resources of 𝐼 𝑚 𝑤 and can only receive collaboration results within 𝑣s authorized scope. Governance primitives. All interactions in the collaboration network are subject to three governance constraints. For any operation 𝑜 performed by identity agent 𝐼 𝑖 𝑢: (1) Identity binding: owner(𝑜) = 𝑢, identity(𝑜) = 𝐼 𝑖 𝑢. Every operation is traceable to specific human and specific identity. (2) Scoped authorization: target(𝑜) 𝜎𝑖. Operations must fall within the agents authorization boundary; violations are intercepted and escalated to user 𝑢. (3) Action-level accountability: every operation is logged as ℓ = (𝑜, 𝑢, 𝐼 𝑖 𝑢 , result, 𝑡) into an append-only audit log ℒ. The concrete enforcement of these primitives at the identity architecture level and during cross-user collaboration is detailed in Section 3. Key Insight Humans as nodes, not agents. Unlike existing multi-agent topologies where agents are the primary actors, ClawNets collaboration network places humans at every node. Agents mediate the edges but never constitute them. Every collaborative relationship is ultimately relationship between two people, governed by their respective authorizations. 2.2. Design Rationale The collaboration network described above requires two complementary infrastructure capabilities. Cross-user collaboration demands that agents remain discoverable and responsive at all times, necessitating that the reasoning engine and identity state persist in the cloud. At the same time, as an OS-level agent, it must also operate within the owners local file system, including reading documents, organizing directories, and managing assets, which requires execution capability on the owners device. ClawNet reconciles these two requirements through the node endpoint mechanism: the users client registers as remote execution node with the cloud, receives operational directives, and executes them on the local file system, forming closed-loop system that bridges cloud-side cognition with edge-side execution. Key Insight Two requirements, two origins. Cloud persistence is driven by the cross-user collaboration paradigm (agents must be always-on to represent their owners). Local execution is inherited from OS-level agent capability (agents must manipulate files on the owners device). Neither subsumes the other. 2.3. Cloud-Side Overview As illustrated in Figure 3 (a), the cloud side comprises two primary components, including gateway containers and central orchestration engine. Gateway containers. To ensure strict multi-tenancy isolation, each user is provisioned with dedicated container running an agent execution engine with OS-level control capabilities. The agent runtime within the container employs think/act loop to drive large language model (LLM)-based reasoning, translating high-level natural language intent into discrete, actionable plans. Each container maintains an independent process space, network stack, and persistent storage, ensuring that the reasoning traces, memory states, and intermediate data of different users remains mutually inaccessible. 𝑀𝑢 and {𝐼1 𝑢} reside within the same 𝑢 , . . . , 𝐼 𝑘 5 ClawNet: Human-Symbiotic Agent Network for Cross-User Autonomous Cooperation Figure 3: (a) High-level architecture of ClawNet. The cloud side encompasses the server (central orchestration engine) and per-user gateway containers (agent runtimes). The edge side consists of the client application, which functions as both user interaction portal and node endpoint for local file system operations. (b) End-to-end data flow for file operation request. Governance mechanisms, comprising identity binding, two-layer scoped authorization (serverside L1 ACL and client-side L2 command policy), and accountability (pre-execution backup and audit logging), are integrated directly into the directive transmission path. container to minimize communication overhead, as 𝑀𝑢 serves as real-time internal advisor to identity agents. Despite co-residence, they operate in logically partitioned workspace subspaces corresponding to the authorization boundaries 𝜎𝑖, preventing cross-domain data leakage (detailed in 3). Server (central orchestration engine). The server acts as the centralized backbone, responsible for identity-based message routing, cross-user collaboration orchestration (3), and the first-layer access control list (L1 ACL) for file operations. It maintains persistent WebSocket connections to each users gateway container. When an agent initiates file operation, the server functions as proxy node, secure mediator between the cloud-side gateway and the edge-side client, verifying that target(𝑜) 𝜎𝑖 before forwarding directives to the users device. 2.4. Node Endpoint Overview The edge side is embodied by the client application installed on the owners device, serving two roles. First, it acts as user interaction portal, providing the graphical interface for dialogue, identity management, collaboration approval, and security event auditing. Second, it acts as node endpoint, the terminal execution environment that receives operational directives from the cloud and performs physical input/output operations on the local file system. Upon initialization, the client registers with the server as node, advertising its supported primitive command set. Figure 3 (b) illustrates the end-to-end data flow of an operational directive. File operation capabilities. The node endpoint equips agents with comprehensive suite of OS-level file primitives, encompassing reading, writing, directory traversal, relocation, renaming, replication, directory creation, and secure deletion. Operating within the think/act loop, agents autonomously orchestrate complex operation sequences to fulfill high-level user intent. The architecture maintains location transparency for the reasoning engine: the agent 6 ClawNet: Human-Symbiotic Agent Network for Cross-User Autonomous Cooperation remains agnostic to the physical execution environment, merely issuing high-level directives to the node for local fulfillment. These capabilities are categorized into read-only operations (e.g., content retrieval, directory listing, and metadata queries) and mutative operations (e.g., writing, moving, and deletion). To ensure system integrity, every mutative action triggers an automated pre-execution backup, ensuring that any modification remains fully traceable and reversible. File access control. Extending OS-level file capabilities to agents inherently exposes the owners most sensitive data to the agentic domain. ClawNet addresses this risk through dual-layer independent authorization scheme, enforcing target(𝑜) 𝜎𝑖 along the entire directive path from cloud to edge. At the first layer (server-side), the server verifies the operation against the identity domain configuration of 𝐼 𝑖 𝑢. At the second layer (client-side), the client independently validates the operation against an explicit folder whitelist defined by the owner. The two layers function autonomously with fail-closed design: rejection by either layer results in full-chain denial. The integration of this scheme with the layered identity architecture, including how different identity agents operate within disparate access scopes and how boundary violations are escalated, is detailed in Section 3. Key Insight Defense in depth, not defense in series. The two authorization layers operate independently. Even if the cloud-side L1 ACL is compromised, the client-side L2 whitelist still enforces the owners boundary. Either layer alone can deny an operation. Operational auditing and reversibility. The node endpoint logs every mutative operation as ℓ = (𝑜, 𝑢, 𝐼 𝑖 𝑢 , result, 𝑡) into an append-only local audit log, with automatic pre-execution backup of all affected files. The owner may invoke single-step undo or batch rollback. File deletion is implemented as relocation to recoverable staging area, and file overwrites automatically preserve the original content. These mechanisms secure the owners sovereign right of correction over the agents autonomous behavior. 3. Identity Governance and Agent Collaboration To credibly collaborate on behalf of its owner, an agent must possess stable and persistent identity. This section builds on the formal definitions in Section 2 to present the concrete mechanisms of identity-centric governance: how agent identity is established through memorydriven cognitive coupling (3.1), the layered architecture of the manager agent and identity agents along with their governance mechanisms (3.2), and the cross-user collaboration protocol built upon this identity governance framework (3.3). 3.1. Identity Binding: From Task Memory to Cognitive Coupling Contemporary agentic systems have begun to incorporate long-term memory, but such capabilities typically focus on operational continuity, preserving user preferences to enhance future task efficiency. ClawNet redefines the role of memory: it serves not merely as tool for task optimization, but as the foundational substrate for an agent to truly know its owner. Key Insight Identity binding is progressive, not configured. The agent builds owner understanding through three layers of memory accumulation: factual memory, pattern memory, and value memory. This progressive cognitive coupling is what transforms generic tool into credible owner proxy (Section 3.1). ClawNet: Human-Symbiotic Agent Network for Cross-User Autonomous Cooperation Specifically, ClawNets memory system achieves cognitive coupling through three progressive layers. The first layer is factual memory. Through daily interactions, users delegate routine tasks via natural language interface, including file organization, document archiving, information retrieval, and directory management. By executing these operations on the users local file system via the node endpoint (2.4), the agent accumulates basic facts about its owner: locations of frequently used files, project directory structures, contact information, and schedules. These facts constitute the raw material of identity cognition. The second layer is pattern memory. As interactions accumulate longitudinally, the agent extracts behavioral patterns from discrete facts: workflow habits (e.g., organizing emails every Monday, archiving financial files at month-end), file organization preferences (e.g., categorizing by project rather than by date), and context-dependent communication styles (e.g., concise and direct with colleagues, polite and formal with clients). Pattern memory enables the agent to anticipate the owners intent rather than passively awaiting instructions. The third layer is value memory. The agent further internalizes the owners decision boundaries and value dispositions: which information must never be shared, what the bottom line is in negotiations, and whether the owner tends to compromise or hold firm when facing conflicts. This value-level understanding enables the agent, when acting as proxy in cross-user collaboration, to know not only what the owner would do but also what the owner would never do. ClawNet persists all three layers of cognition across sessions. The agent does not experience amnesia at the conclusion of session but incrementally deepens its alignment with the owner through each successive engagement. Consequently, the relationship between the agent and its owner transcends technical isolation (dedicated containers and segregated workspaces) to achieve cognitive coupling: the agent forms unique, irreplaceable personalized model of its owner. This cognitive coupling is the critical foundation for the agents evolution from generic utility into sovereign representative. 3.2. Layered Identity Architecture Once identity binding is established, critical design question arises: should user be represented by single monolithic agent or distributed set of specialized agents? monolithic agent suffers from an inherent tension: deep owner-alignment is essential for effective proxying, but it significantly elevates the risk of inadvertent privacy disclosure during external interactions. Conversely, constraining an agents knowledge for security purposes inevitably compromises its capacity for cross-domain reasoning. ClawNet reconciles this conflict through layered identity architecture, partitioning the users agent system 𝒜𝑢 = (𝑀𝑢 , {𝐼1 Key Insight 𝑢}) into two distinct tiers. 𝑢 , . . . , 𝐼 𝑘 Cannot beats should not. monolithic agent that knows everything about its owner is powerful but dangerous: prompt-level constraints cannot reliably prevent privacy leakage. The layered architecture resolves this by letting 𝑀𝑢 hold complete cognition but remain architecturally unexposed, while each 𝐼 𝑖 𝑢 faces the outside world carrying only context-relevant knowledge. Security shifts from behavioral reliability to structural determinism (Section 3.2). Manager agent. 𝑀𝑢 serves as the authoritative anchor of the users digital identity. It has the privilege to access the memory contents of all identity agents {𝐼1 𝑢}, enabling it to form cross-domain global perspective. Crucially, 𝑀𝑢 does not maintain separate global knowledge base; rather, it derives its global understanding through aggregated access to all identity agents memories. 𝑢 , . . . , 𝐼 𝑘 8 ClawNet: Human-Symbiotic Agent Network for Cross-User Autonomous Cooperation This global access capability makes 𝑀𝑢s direct participation in external interactions inherently risky. Consider scenario where 𝑀𝑢 can simultaneously access confidential commercial contract information stored in the work identity agent and private health records stored in the personal identity agent. If 𝑀𝑢 is queried with how have you been lately? during collaborative session with colleague, it might inadvertently leak health information in its response. Prompt-level security constraints such as do not share private information rely on the model perfectly adhering to instructions across every inference step, requirement that is statistically impossible to guarantee. ClawNet therefore enforces architectural-level isolation on 𝑀𝑢: the collaboration protocol is structurally incapable of routing external messages to 𝑀𝑢. The security paradigm shifts from policy-based should not (relying on model behavioral reliability) to an architectural cannot (leveraging system-level determinism). Under this isolation constraint, 𝑀𝑢 assumes the role of an internal consultant. When 𝐼 𝑖 𝑢 requires cross-domain knowledge support during collaboration, it may solicit guidance from 𝑀𝑢. 𝑀𝑢 provides advice based on its aggregated access to all identity agents memories, subject to two constraints: 𝑀𝑢s response is never sent directly to external collaborators but is instead presented to 𝐼 𝑖 𝑢 and the owner, who decide whether and how to integrate it; and 𝑀𝑢 is guided by 𝐼 𝑖 𝑢s current context 𝑐𝑖 when formulating advice, avoiding unsolicited exposure of information from unrelated domains. Identity agents. Each 𝐼 𝑖 𝑢 = (𝑐𝑖 , 𝜎𝑖 , 𝒦𝑖 , 𝒫𝑖) is contextual projection of the owner within specific collaborative environment. Individuals fulfill diverse social roles across different settings: project manager in professional context, parent in family life, researcher in academia. Each identity agent represents digital instantiation of one such role, bound to an identity domain marked by tag, with an independent knowledge space 𝒦𝑖 𝒦𝑢 and persistent memory. In implementation, different identity agents operate within logically isolated workspace 𝑢 can only access files and resources 𝑢 (𝑖 𝑗), even though they belong to subspaces inside the same gateway container. Each 𝐼 𝑖 𝑢 cannot read the workspace contents of 𝐼 𝑗 within 𝜎𝑖, and 𝐼 𝑖 the same owner. The core value of identity agents is realized in cross-user collaboration. The owner explicitly selects which identity to present when engaging with another users agent: work identity agent to coordinate project milestones with colleague, or an academic identity agent to discuss research methodologies with collaborator. The selected agent carries only the knowledge 𝒦𝑖 of its identity domain, ensuring that information from other domains remains entirely inaccessible to the counterpart. The prerogative to select the active identity resides exclusively with the owner. The lifecycle of an identity agent evolves in synchrony with the owners social roles: instantiated when new role emerges (with 𝒫𝑖 and 𝜎𝑖 configured), retired when the role concludes (with authorizations and records archived), and its parameters adjustable at any time. Key Insight Complementary roles. 𝑀𝑢 and {𝐼 𝑖 𝑢} form governed agency system. 𝑀𝑢 is the unified internal cognition, able to access all identities memories but never externally exposed. Each 𝐼 𝑖 𝑢 is an outward-facing social persona, specialized but controlled. Together they make deep owner understanding and safe external collaboration no longer contradictory (Section 3.2). Identity-level authorization and accountability. The layered identity architecture provides 𝑢s file access scope is conclear anchors for both authorization and accountability. Each 𝐼 𝑖 9 ClawNet: Human-Symbiotic Agent Network for Cross-User Autonomous Cooperation strained by 𝜎𝑖, enforced through the dual-layer authorization scheme described in Section 2.4. Authorization policies evolve dynamically with the owners real-world social roles. The architectural isolation of 𝑀𝑢 constitutes an additional authorization boundary: it holds the most comprehensive access privileges but is structurally barred from external communication. Every operation is bound to the originating identity agents identifier and its identity domain, logged as ℓ = (𝑜, 𝑢, 𝐼 𝑖 𝑢 , result, 𝑡). The owner can precisely trace the provenance and authorization basis of any action. Unauthorized access attempts are automatically intercepted and dispatched as boundary violation events to the owners security event center, ensuring that every breach is audited and cannot be ignored. 3.3. Cross-User Collaboration The preceding sections described identity governance within single user domain. This section extends the scope to the inter-user dimension: the mechanisms governing collaboration between identity agents belonging to different owners. Key Insight Agents automate the process, not the decision. In cross-user collaboration, agents handle information exchange, solution deliberation, and progress synchronization. But the authority to initiate collaboration, select the active identity, delineate information-sharing boundaries, and make final decisions remains with the human owner (Section 3.3). Establishing contact relationships and identity assignment. The prerequisite for collaboration is contact relationship between two users. user sends friend request; upon confirmation, they become contacts. The user then designates which identity agent to present to each contact: work identity agent for colleague, an academic identity agent for collaborating advisor. When facing different counterparts, the same user presents distinct identity projections, each carrying its own 𝒦𝑖 and 𝜎𝑖. Users can dynamically adjust identity assignments or remove contacts at any time. The construction of the collaboration network is entirely in the hands of the user. Collaboration initiation and bilateral approval. collaborative interaction is triggered when 𝐼 𝑖 𝑢 manifests the intent to communicate with another user during local conversation, for instance, determining the need to retrieve information from colleague. The system does not autonomously initiate the collaboration. Instead, it intercepts the action and issues an authorization request to the owner. The request is dispatched to the counterpart only after securing the owners explicit approval. The counterparts owner must also approve. collaboration session is established only when both parties provide explicit consent: approve(𝑢, 𝐼 𝑖 𝑢) approve(𝑣, 𝐼 𝑗 𝑣) 𝑢 𝒫𝑗 𝑣 𝒫𝑖 rejection by either party immediately terminates the initiation sequence. Without this mandatory bilateral approval, an agent might unilaterally share information without the owners awareness, an outcome that is fundamentally unacceptable when the interacting agents represent distinct owners with independent interests. Multi-turn dialogue and autonomous collaboration. Once the session is established, the identity agents of both parties enter an autonomous multi-turn dialogue. Agents exchange information, deliberate on solutions, and orchestrate tasks, each generating responses strictly based on its own 𝒦𝑖. At every turn, the server injects role-specific system prompts that clearly define each agents identity, stance, and behavioral norms, preventing the well-known problem of role drift in multi-turn LLM conversations. The server simultaneously performs identity verification and authorization checks. 10 ClawNet: Human-Symbiotic Agent Network for Cross-User Autonomous Cooperation Conversation termination is governed by three layers of control. At the agent layer, the agent evaluates dialogue progression through semantic state markers and gracefully concludes the conversation upon achieving its objective. At the system layer, maximum turn threshold prevents indefinite continuation. At the owner layer, the human own 4. Related Work Single-agent and multi-agent frameworks. Single-agent systems such as OpenClaw (OpenClaw Community, 2026) and Anthropics Computer Use (Anthropic, 2024) have advanced agent capability from tool augmentation to OS-level control, but address only what one agent can do for one user. Multi-agent frameworks such as MetaGPT (Hong et al., 2023), AutoGen (Wu et al., 2024), CrewAI (cre, 2024), ChatDev (Qian et al., 2024), and CAMEL (Li et al., 2023) demonstrate effective collaboration among multiple agents through role-playing and structured workflows. However, all operate within single principals scope: agents share the same user, the same goal, and the same trust domain. When MetaGPT assigns agents the roles of Product Manager and Engineer, these are functional specializations, not representatives of different humans with divergent interests. ClawNet addresses fundamentally different setting: each agent is permanently bound to distinct human owner, and cross-user cooperation takes the form of governed inter-agent negotiation. Agent safety and governance. growing body of work recognizes the governance gap in agentic systems. (Shavit et al., 2023) outline practices for governing agentic AI but do not provide system implementation. (Chan et al., 2025) identify the need for infrastructure supporting agent identity and interaction, and (South et al., 2025) propose authenticated delegation as prerequisite for authorized AI agents. (Casper et al., 2025) catalog the landscape of AI agents and their accountability gaps, while (de Witt, 2025) discuss the structural challenges of multi-agent security. These works collectively establish the theoretical motivation for governance-aware agent systems but stop short of an end-to-end implementation. ClawNet translates these identified requirements into deployed system with concrete enforcement mechanisms. Agent interoperability protocols. Standardized agent communication has long history, from KQML (Finin et al., 1994) to FIPA Agent Communication Language (FIPA-ACL) (Pitt & Mamdani, 1999). More recently, Googles Agent2Agent protocol (Surapaneni et al., 2025) provides communication layer for agents to discover and exchange messages across framework boundaries, and Anthropics Model Context Protocol enables standardized tool integration. These protocols address how agents communicate but not under what governance: they do not bind agents to specific owners, enforce authorization scopes, or produce auditable responsibility chains. ClawNet can be viewed as governance layer that operates on top of such communication protocols, adding identity binding, scoped authorization, and action-level accountability to the interaction substrate. 5. Conclusion We have presented ClawNet, an identity-governed agent collaboration framework that instantiates human-symbiotic paradigm for cross-user autonomous cooperation. By permanently binding each agent to specific human owner through progressive cognitive coupling, partitioning identity into privacy-preserving and outward-facing tiers, and enforcing identity binding, scoped authorization, and action-level accountability throughout every interaction, ClawNet moves beyond the digitization of individual skills toward the digitization of human collaborative relationships themselves. 11 ClawNet: Human-Symbiotic Agent Network for Cross-User Autonomous Cooperation We believe this work points to broader shift in how AI agents should be designed. As agents grow more capable and autonomous, they will inevitably need to interact with agents belonging to other people, organizations, and institutions. The governance infrastructure for such interactions cannot be an afterthought. Just as the internet required protocols for identity and trust before e-commerce could flourish, the emerging ecosystem of autonomous agents requires governance primitives before cross-user agent collaboration can become practical reality. We hope that ClawNet serves as both working prototype and an open invitation for the community to build upon this foundation.
